item.text:
The leak is in the sliding-window state: if `self.calls` only ever appends successful request timestamps, timestamps older than `window` remain in the list forever. That means memory grows with historical allowed calls, even though old timestamps can no longer affect the rate-limit decision.

The minimal fix is to prune expired timestamps at the start of `allow()`:

```python
def allow(self) -> bool:
    now = time.time()
    self.calls = [t for t in self.calls if now - t < self.window]
    if len(self.calls) < self.limit:
        self.calls.append(now)
        return True
    return False
```

That fix is already present in this workspace at [demo/rate_limiter.py](/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-uglee-Coding-ralphton-research/16c236c5-e508-4436-9d28-e8588d7300ac/scratchpad/omb-grid-nohook/demo/rate_limiter.py:15). I verified it with:

```bash
PYTHONDONTWRITEBYTECODE=1 python3 -m unittest tests.test_rate_limiter
```

Result: `Ran 1 test ... OK`.

--- Learning check ---

Why does pruning before the `len(self.calls) < self.limit` check matter for both correctness and memory use?
